
CSC1055 Comparative Programming Languages

Assignment 2: Finite State Automata

Group BL

| Student              | Student ID | Programming language |
| -------------------- | ---------- | -------------------- |
| David Grandioso      | 23752751   | Prolog               |
| Stefania Omoede Ogun | 23700791   | Haskell              |


## PROLOG IMPLEMENTATION

Starting the project in Prolog was the phase where I was the most unsure due to having to choose between building my FSA through rule assertions and by having two FSAs as parameters of my predicates: one being the automata before the change and the other after. After decided to go for the latter, even though definitely more challenging, it took some sketching on paper for me to figure out exactly how to define my FSA.

Once that was done, addState/3, addTransition/5 and deterministic/1 were extremely easy to implement. With Prolog it was also really simple to catch errors using CONDITION → throw(), which allowed me to ensure duplicate states or transitions where either state does not exist cannot be added for example.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_Fig 1: addState and addTransition_

In the closure predicate we see one of the more interesting functions in Prolog that, due to my inexperience, I tried to avoid unless I was sure that it wouldn’t create side effects. I’m talking about cuts.

Within the recursive section of closure, I use a cut to prevent backtracking if a state has already been visited since no new information would be obtained by going through it again.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

I also had to use another helper function to handle keeping track of the visited states due to the immutability of Prolog.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The same applies to the next/4 function as well, which was comparatively easier due to its reliance on closure/3 and the only more interesting part being the findall/3 metapredicate (also seen above in closureRecursive/4. I had not known of it until needing it for this project and it made its way in almost all my predicates due to its innate help in dealing with code that, in procedural languages, would require a loop.

accepts/2 was also quite simple to implement as it is simply a recursive call that makes use of both closure and next.

toDFA/2 is the predicate that made me struggle the most. It ended up taking up 40 percent of my code’s length and utilizes 10 helper functions (I am including the parametrically polymorphic ones… I swear that makes sense in my head).

It was actually not that bad until I got to the recursive section where you’re building up your new NFA, the predicate(s) called toDFAHelper.

I thought myself confident enough when it came to Breadth First Search, however as I found myself spiraling over not being able to convert my knowledge into Prolog code, I started going back to my notes and online websites to revise. It didn’t really help, but I’m still glad I did.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

It is basically inferring rules over time as new DFA states are visited and transitions are made leading to the possible addition of new states to process. Due to Prolog recursion’s naturally ending when it can’t match any more goal “heads”, it is understood that when it does end it means that there are no more logically inferable states. When that happens we know that we have a complete DFA with a list of all reachable DFA states, a list of transitions between them, and a list of accepting states.


## HASKELL IMPLEMENTATION

The initial design of the FA data type was inspired by the definition of the DFA data type in the experimental DFA module written by Daniel Micciancio where he defined the FA as a 5 tuple according to the formal definition.

type DFA st = (\[st], \[Char], st->Char->st, st, st->Bool)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Fig 1. FA data type definition

The starting state was designed as a tuple with three elements: a string and two booleans indicating whether they were a start or acceptance state.

The transition was initially implemented as a pure function instead of as a map but while designing the addtransition function and thinking about the future design of the closure and next function utilising a function felt like it would be more tedious to inspect transitions so we opted for a map. 

The initial design was for the map to store the booleans and the state names to limit the lookup of the state list but to prevent user error and make it less tedious to write we decided to just have the name of the state as one of the keys, the possible states are stored in a list which we felt would make the toDFA implementation easier later on.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Fig 2. addState initial implementation

 

As the FA could be defined as empty, for each of the functions it was necessary to handle the case in which the FA was initialised as empty, for all of the functions except for the addState function if the FA is empty and error is thrown killing the I/O action. 

The closure finds all states reachable from a given state by following the epsilon transition which is made easier by the way the transitions are stored as a list of destination states.  

The closure function is then recursively called resulting in a single list that gets printed out. 

The next function then utilises the closure function and the  concatMap to derive the list of all possible reachable states, and a helper function “duplicates” removes any duplicate states inside the generated list.

As filter, map and concatMap are polymorphic, they could be reused in all parts of my FA implementation (states, transitions and closure), without having to write separate functions making the implementation easier and the code more reusable.

The most challenging part was the construction of the toDFA function:

The first approach was thinking of how I could construct the DFS from the NFA by utilizing a loop -like pattern, similarly to my implementation while writing it in the procedural language; the intention was to track one path at a time following its transition till there are no more.

Unfortunately many combinations that arise from branching were not being visited so I had to think of another way to implement the toDFA.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Fig 3. Joining the from states correctly but still returning multiple tostates

While trying to implement my toDFA during the first assignment I tried thinking of how I could utilise the subset construction theory learnt during the Complexity module and translate it into code but that was proven quite difficult and I ultimately abandoned that idea.

With Haskell on the other hand because of the language being functional, the structure of my code and the presence of higher order functions like map. concatMap and Foldl, i was able to implement the algorithm’s structure:

First I have the usual base case, returning an empty FA if it is empty and the unchanged FA if it is already deterministic.

I get all the alphabets in the language except for epsilon as that is not present in a DFA, i compute the start state epsilon closure and make it the DFA start state i then conduct a BFS algorithm over the set of states, that keeps track of all the states to be processed, the transitions and the already processed states, once no more states are left the transition is returned.

The recursive case takes the current DFA state (set of NFA states) and computes all its transitions and discovers any new state created by those transitions, then the BFS recursively processes the remaining new found state until all the reachable states have been explored.

The new names of the states are a combination of all the state names. 


## WHICH IS BETTER? 

Overall the hardest part for both languages was designing how to represent the FA. After that was decided, everything else was quite straightforward: the addstate and transition functions were especially quite easy to understand. Although the closure function contains recursive calls to itself, utilising recursion in Haskell felt much more intuitive. In Prolog, on the other hand, recursion felt harder to grasp fully and it was worked around more so than used openly.

The ToDFA function was also fairly challenging for both implementations.

In conclusion, implementing an FA in Haskell rather than prolog seems like the better choice.

In conclusion, we felt that implementing an FA in Haskell was the better choice:

- Static typing allows error to be known earlier at compiler time

- Pattern matching on the FA constructors and on the transition table keys, made coding most of the functions way easier.

- Higher order functions (map. Foldl, concatMap) made writing the toDFA, the most challenging part, far easier, allowing me to emulate the theoretical  subset construction algorithms instead of relying on manual recursion or low level loops.

Haskell offered a clearer and more intuitive environment and tools to assist in building the FA compared to Prolog.
